title: Provable In-Context Learning of Nonlinear Regression with Transformers
authors: Not specified in the provided text
abstract: This paper develops a theoretical framework for in-context learning (ICL) with nonlinear regression functions using transformers. The authors analyze the training dynamics of transformers under gradient descent to understand how these models can learn nonlinear functions without parameter updates.
contribution_summary: The paper generalizes previous studies by analyzing a broader class of nonlinear functions (L-Lipschitz task functions) and more general feature sets, providing theoretical insights into how transformers can learn nonlinear regression tasks through in-context learning.
research_questions: What salient geometric properties of the target function govern the convergence behavior of transformer-based learning?
Can transformers learn effectively in context and achieve globally small prediction error with nonlinear functions?
methodology: Analysis of training dynamics of transformers under gradient descent, focusing on one-layer transformers with attention mechanisms.
related_work: Studies on ICL with linear functions [18, 19]
Investigation of attention dynamics during training [18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23]
key_findings: Transformers can learn nonlinear regression functions through in-context learning
The geometric properties of target functions influence convergence behavior
One-layer transformers with attention mechanisms can achieve globally small prediction error with nonlinear functions
